title: Patient Blood Management: a systematic review of current international guidelines
one_line_summary:
- **Systematic review** of **40** national and institutional **PBM guidelines**  
- Found **substantial agreement** on **restrictive transfusion thresholds**  
- Noted **marked variability** in **strategies to reduce diagnostic blood loss**  
- Highlights **fragmented** and **incomplete global adoption**
key_pearls: Only 13 nations (including 9 high-income countries) have formally established national PBM programmes encompassing all three pillars, despite 25+ years of advocacy.

Restrictive transfusion thresholds are broadly agreed upon, with 22 guidelines defining a **haemoglobin threshold of 7 g dl⁻¹** or lower, though transfusion is not **mandatory** if adequate compensatory mechanisms exist.

Greatest variability across guidelines concerns strategies to reduce **diagnostic blood loss** and definitions of **intervention-specific bleeding risk**, with many documents providing no explicit recommendations.
Guidance for acute care and intensive care is usually issued by specialty societies rather than national authorities; only one pan-European trauma guideline includes prehospital PBM elements.
Most guidelines recommend **oral iron** as first-line therapy for iron deficiency, reserving **intravenous iron** for malabsorption, intolerance, or clinical need for rapid repletion.
National standardisation is frequently absent even in countries with advanced hospital-level PBM programmes, with guidelines remaining fragmented and lacking comprehensive policy frameworks.
Adopting living guideline models and keeping national frameworks general with specialty-specific supplementary recommendations are proposed to address inter-guideline variability.
population: National and institutional PBM guidelines from 40 countries/organisations, including governmental, ministerial, and professional society documents

sections.heading: Introduction and Background
sections.content:
- Patient Blood Management (PBM) is a patient-centred, systematic, evidence-based approach to improve outcomes by managing and preserving a patient's own blood.
- PBM is based on three pillars: 1) optimising red cell mass, 2) minimising blood loss, and 3) harnessing and optimising physiological tolerance of anaemia.
- The WHO and European Commission have repeatedly called for national-level implementation of PBM strategies.
- By 2025, global uptake remains inconsistent; only 59% of WHO member states have a national strategic plan for blood safety.
- Anaemia is highly prevalent, affecting an estimated **1.95–2.36 billion** individuals worldwide in 2021, particularly in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs).
- The review aimed to assess the global status of national PBM frameworks, compare existing recommendations, and evaluate alignment with WHO principles.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- Systematic review conducted per PRISMA 2020 statement; protocol registered on PROSPERO (**CRD420251032664**).
- Searched PubMed, Cochrane Library, and Google Scholar in March 2024 for publications from the past 20 years.
- Inclusion criteria: enshrined in national legislation, developed with governmental involvement, based on systematic review by relevant working groups, national/territorial applicability, most recent version.
- Documents screened for coverage of predefined PBM elements categorised by the three WHO-defined pillars.
- Guideline quality assessed using the **iCAHE Guideline Quality Checklist** (14 binary items); threshold for inclusion set at ≥10/14 (≈71%).
- Legal texts considered in original form without formal quality assessment.
- Non-English guidelines translated using DeepL or Google Translate.
- Recommendations colour-coded into seven predefined categories for comparative analysis.

sections.heading: Results: Global Landscape and Scope
sections.content:
- **40 documents** included for qualitative analysis: 22 developed with national ministry of health involvement, some formally integrated into legislation.
- Australia leads with six national guideline modules widely referenced internationally.
- In Europe, national programmes exist in France, Great Britain, Italy, and Romania; limited programmes in Austria, Poland, and the Netherlands.
- Italy was the first European country with official ministerial PBM support.
- In Africa, national PBM-related programmes identified only in Ghana; South Africa actively developing protocols.
- Across America, Mexico has established a national PBM programme; protocols developing in Canada and the USA.
- Most guidelines focus on adult elective surgical patients; recommendations for internal medicine, paediatric, obstetric, acute care, or intensive care populations are inconsistently represented.
- Guidance for in-hospital acute care generally issued by anaesthesiology or intensive care societies.
- Only one pan-European trauma guideline (ABC-T) includes elements relevant to the prehospital setting.

sections.heading: Results: Pillar 1 - Optimising Red Cell Mass
sections.content:
- Majority of guidelines provide comprehensive recommendations on perioperative anaemia diagnostics and treatment.
- Several organisations (ACOI/SIAARTI/SIMTI, SASA, European Commission, Italy) explicitly recommend establishing **anaemia clinics** to optimise patients before elective surgery.
- Specific protocols for postponing elective procedures until anaemia is corrected vary widely and are often lacking.
- For iron deficiency: most documents recommend **oral iron** as first-line therapy; **intravenous iron** reserved for contraindications, intolerance, malabsorption, functional iron deficiency, or need for rapid repletion.
- A minority recommend intravenous iron as first-line therapy; in pregnancy and postpartum period, intravenous iron is frequently advised.
- Recommendations regarding EPO, ESAs, and vitamin supplementation show greater variability.
- CCSSA **critically** evaluates routine use of EPO or iron in **critically** ill anaemic patients given lack of clear **mortality** benefit and infection burden.

sections.heading: Results: Pillar 2 - Minimising Blood Loss and Bleeding
sections.content:
- Marked heterogeneity observed in recommendations concerning reduction of **diagnostic blood loss** (targeted sampling, small-volume collection, noninvasive monitoring).
- Some authors consider impact of diagnostic blood loss on outcomes in adult patients outside ICU negligible; guidelines encompassing paediatric and ICU patients vary.
- Definition of **surgery-related bleeding risk** often unspecified.
- Only a few guidelines address blood-conserving surgical techniques (minimally invasive circuits, off-pump CABG, video-assisted procedures, electrocautery, tourniquets, avoidance of routine suction drainage).
- Guidance on **POCT** including viscoelastic haemostatic assays consistently reported across documents.
- Routine **perioperative autologous donation (PAD)** not **recommended**; selected indications include cardiac surgery, rare blood group phenotypes, complex alloimmunisation.
- Most documents include clinical and organisational frameworks for **massive haemorrhage protocols**.
- Several guidelines recommend intraoperative permissive hypotension or cautious vasopressor use to reduce bleeding.
- Management of perioperative antithrombotic therapy and coagulopathy has gained prominence.

sections.heading: Results: Pillar 3 - Harnessing Physiological Reserve of Anaemia
sections.content:
- Broad agreement that transfusion decisions should not be based solely on Hb/Hct values but reflect comprehensive clinical assessment including physiological reserve and expected disease trajectory.
- Restrictive transfusion thresholds consistently **recommended**; for adults, **Hb threshold of 7 g dl⁻¹** or lower frequently cited (22 guidelines at **7 g** dl⁻¹, nine guidelines lower).
- Several guidelines (Mexico, Romania, DPR Korea, Germany, Austria, Poland, ESAIC, ASA, ÖGARI) emphasise that even at these thresholds, transfusion is not **mandatory** if adequate compensatory mechanisms are present.
- For neonates and infants, higher threshold values suggested depending on clinical condition and need for organ support.
- If a general upper transfusion limit specified for adults, usually set at **10 g dl⁻¹ Hb**.
- In acute brain injury and ACS, thresholds generally set slightly higher; NBA recommends **8 g dl⁻¹** for ACS.
- Recommendations for **single-unit transfusion policy** vary across institutions; reflected in Choosing Wisely initiatives ('Why give two when one will do?').
- Beyond RBC transfusion, guidelines specify indications for plasma, cryoprecipitate, factor concentrates, platelets, and white blood cells, primarily **recommended** for restrictive use.

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- Substantial gap identified in availability of formally established, independent national PBM programmes encompassing all three pillars, even in HICs.
- Highest concordance observed for third pillar recommendations, followed by first pillar; greatest variability in second pillar measures.
- National transfusion guidelines more likely than PBM-specific documents to include recommendations on plasma, cryoprecipitate, factor concentrates, platelets, and WBC transfusions.
- Variation reflects complex, interdisciplinary nature of PBM; geopolitical, cultural, and health system factors contribute to heterogeneity.
- Three proposed approaches to address discrepancies: 1) keep national framework guidelines general with specialty-specific supplements; 2) develop national guideline with all relevant professional associations (risks excessively long documents); 3) adopt **living guideline models** to mitigate publication date differences.
- Only one guideline refers to PBM in the prehospital setting, highlighting a major knowledge gap.
- Implementation in LMICs remains constrained by inadequate infrastructure, insufficient funding, and deficits in training and continuing professional education.

sections.heading: Strengths and Limitations
sections.content:
- Strengths: systematic review methodology with PRISMA adherence; comprehensive search including governmental and institutional sources; use of validated iCAHE quality assessment tool; inclusion of non-English guidelines via translation.
- Limitations: systematic literature review rather than direct survey of national ministries may limit completeness; analysis limited to predefined PBM elements introducing potential selection bias; most documents from HICs and published in English raising language and publication bias; several included guidelines date back to 2010 and are under revision; country income-level bias remains important consideration.

strengths_limitations: - Strengths: systematic review methodology with PRISMA adherence; comprehensive search including governmental and institutional sources; use of validated iCAHE quality assessment tool; inclusion of non-English guidelines via translation. - Limitations: systematic literature review rather than direct survey of national ministries may limit completeness; analysis limited to predefined PBM elements introducing potential selection bias; most documents from HICs and published in English raising language and publication bias; several included guidelines date back to 2010 and are under revision; country income-level bias remains important consideration.
